Italy presents a striking paradox: it combines the OECD's most extensive collective bargaining coverage with persistent real wage stagnation, high income inequality and rising in-work poverty. Understanding this divergence requires examining the contents of Italy's collective bargaining agreements (CBAs). Yet the texts of Italy's thousands of CBAs, archived by the National Council for Economics and Labour (CNEL), are stored as scanned PDFs that cannot be analysed at scale. We address this gap by scraping all 2,512 definitive agreements from the CNEL repository and converting them into searchable Markdown files using optical character recognition techniques. We then demonstrate the corpus's analytical potential through a dictionary-based analysis of how the salience of different topics has evolved across economic sectors and over time. We find that the importance of wage indexation and cost-of-living adjustments has steadily receded while social investment and the safeguard against "new social risks" have become increasingly prominent objects of negotiation. By opening these texts to systematic analysis, CCNL-Open offers researchers a new resource for studying the substance of what social partners negotiate.

title: Drafting and Free-Riding in Mass-Start Triathlon: Evidence from a COVID-19 Start-Format Discontinuity
abstract: Drafting in the swim leg of mass-start triathlon transfers a hydrodynamic cost advantage to the bike and run, where it surfaces as a change in relative rank. I use cross-stage rank improvement as a reverse proxy for free-riding and study its determinants in a ten-year panel of athlete × event records centred on 2020, when COVID-19 response policies replaced mass starts with individually staggered starts and mechanically curtailed drafting. A compact two-stage contest model, in which drafting enters the bike–run stage as a multiplicative cost shifter, organises the predictions. Identification combines pooled OLS with athlete and event fixed effects and a sharp regression-discontinuity design at the 2019/2020 cut-off. Free-riding was substantial before 2020, fell sharply in 2020 (a local-linear RDD estimate of about −31 rank places; a local-cubic estimate of about −92), and recovered only partially afterwards. The gains are largest for weaker swimmers and deeper drafting positions, the gender gap is modest, and they rise with swim-group size at an estimated log–log elasticity of about 0.43. The 2020 disruption thus acts as a contraction of the feasible set for free-riding, and its recovery is uneven rather than broad-based.

title: Effects of poverty conceptions on design for the Resource-constrained populations
abstract: Design for the resource-constrained population aims to address the negative effects of its defining attribute: poverty. A designer must be able to answer fundamental questions, such as how to decide who is poor, why poverty in one context differs from others, which types of projects could affect which aspects of poverty, etc. Poverty studies and developmental economics have a scientific understanding of poverty, which is inaccessible to designers due to differences in the nomenclature. This paper addresses this gap by interlinking the five levels of poverty that deal with the conception of unfairness in poverty and related ethical arguments, its causes, its measures, and the possible remedies by linking it with the three design elements, namely, the designer, the design problem, and the design process, resulting in five extensions about the scope, execution, and evaluation of the projects, and the fundamental skills and abilities of the designer.

title: An Interdisciplinary Research Framework for Social Media and Youth Health
abstract: Policymakers, schools, families and clinicians are increasingly looking to take action to protect young people from potential harms associated with social media use. Australia’s national ban on social media accounts for people under the age of 16 represents one of the largest-scale interventions to date, and similar bans are now under consideration in jurisdictions around the world. Despite public and political support for such interventions, evidence of their effectiveness, and the impact of social media on young people’s health more broadly, is limited and contested. Causal mechanisms, platform-specific effects, mediating factors and the effectiveness of various interventions are not well understood. This white paper presents an interdisciplinary research framework for social media and youth health to address these gaps, developed through a collaboration between the University of Sydney and Yale University. Drawing on seventeen fields — including neuroscience, clinical psychology, law and policy, platform and algorithmic studies, and family and youth studies — the framework identifies four core research dimensions (causation, platforms, contexts, and interventions) and six outcome domains for evidence-based action.

title: Smallholder farmers urgently need improved solar dryers as a catalyst for agricultural innovation
abstract: Nepal's smallholder farmers, constituting over 66% of the agricultural workforce face post-harvest losses of 20–40% across major crops, representing billions of nepali rupees in preventable annual waste. This opinion paper argues that improved solar dryers are not a tangential technology but a structural necessity and a catalyst for broader agricultural innovation in Nepal's unique socioeconomic, climatic and geographic context. Based on evidence from comparable agrarian economies, this paper examines the investment case for solar drying technology and calls for its prioritization within Nepal's national agri-innovation agenda. The intersection of Nepal's abundant solar radiation, high post-harvest loss rates, market access constraints, and escalating climate variability creates an undeniable imperative for scaled, policy-backed deployment of cost-effective solar drying solutions.

title: Militants in Exile: Spanish Republicans and Political Mobilization in France
authors: Alejandro López-Peceño, Pau Grau-Vilalta, Elias Dinas
abstract: Can political exiles reshape the politics of the societies that receive them? Existing research on the political effects of migration has largely focused on how native populations react to migrants' presence rather than on migrants' capacity to shape local politics directly. We argue that political exiles, who often arrive with strong political identities and organizational experience, can instead act as agents of horizontal transmission within host societies. We examine this argument by studying the exile of nearly two hundred thousand Spanish Republicans to France following the Spanish Civil War. Leveraging newly digitized refugee records and a difference-in-differences design, we show that communes hosting larger numbers of refugees shifted significantly toward the French Communist Party (PCF) after World War II. We provide evidence that this effect partly operated through Resistance mobilization: communes receiving more Spanish Republicans experienced greater Resistance activity, which helped legitimize the PCF in the postwar period.

title: Psychologie politique et ingérences étrangères. Pourquoi nos esprits rendent les démocraties vulnérables aux opérations d'influence informationnelle ?
abstract: Les opérations d'influence étrangère sont généralement analysées sous l'angle géopolitique ou technique. Cet article propose une lecture complémentaire, ancrée dans la psychologie politique : les ingérences étrangères ne créent pas les vulnérabilités démocratiques, elles en exploitent les fondations cognitives, et sont en ce sens, « intuitives ». En mobilisant la recherche sur le raisonnement motivé, la polarisation affective, l'identité sociale et la pensée tribale, et les théories naïves des relations internationales, nous montrons que les opérations d'influence les plus efficaces s'articulent précisément sur des biais cognitifs universels et des fractures sociales préexistantes. Cette lecture permet d'identifier pourquoi les sociétés démocratiques sont vulnérables en général, pourquoi certaines sociétés sont plus résilientes que d'autres, et quelles interventions psychosociales et métacognitives pourraient renforcer la résistance collective aux manipulations informationnelles.

title: example-project: A Reproducible Empirical Research Template
abstract: An open-source teaching hub and template suite for reproducible empirical research, primarily targeting empirical business researchers in accounting and finance. Pairs with companion repositories project-template (the research pipeline) and overleaf-template (the LaTeX manuscript).

title: The Political Benefits of the Monoculture: Estimating the Electoral Effect of the Market Facilitation Program
abstract: Does the distribution of government transfers affect elections? We analyze a natural experiment in the 2019 wave of the US Department of Agriculture's Market Facilitation Program (MFP). The 2019 MFP allocated $14.5 billion via a formula combining historical production data and commodity-specific trade damages. We show how a methodological quirk resulted in arbitrary variation in these damages that propagated through the formula into excessive county-level compensation rates. We estimate the effects of this payment shock using a novel, design-based, randomization inference approach to account for complex dependencies across US counties. We find that counties receiving greater compensation rates, on average, have higher two-party Republican presidential vote shares in the 2020 election. Instrumenting for actual 2019 MFP disbursements, we find an additional $1 million in payments to a county increased that county's 2020 two-party Trump vote share by about .18 percentage points on average. Had the 2019 MFP maintained the spending levels of the 2018 wave, we estimate that Candidate Trump's two-party vote share would have been .117 percentage points lower nationally, with particularly pronounced effects in swing states like Arizona (.41 percentage points) and Georgia (.159 percentage points).

title: The Dynamics of Competitive Intervention: Multiplicity, Rivalry, and Civil War Duration
abstract: Civil wars rarely involve only two self-contained combatants. Instead, they often attract multiple external actors whose competing interests complicate efforts to reach a settlement. While previous research has shown that interventions on both sides of a conflict tend to prolong civil wars, it has largely overlooked the multiplicity of such interventions and, in particular, the relationships that intervening states maintain with their counterparts supporting the opposing side. We address this gap by introducing the concept of the competitive intervener dyad (CID), defined as a pair of external states that simultaneously support opposing sides in a civil war. We argue that a greater number of CIDs prolongs conflict by exacerbating both the information and commitment problems that hinder bargaining between warring parties. Importantly, these bargaining problems become even more severe when CIDs consist of interstate rivals. In such cases, entrenched mistrust and conflicting interests make accurate assessments and credible commitments especially difficult to sustain. To test these propositions, we combine global data on civil wars from 1975 to 2017 with measures of external support and interstate rivalry and estimate Cox proportional hazards models. The results indicate that a higher number of CIDs significantly reduces the likelihood of civil war termination. Moreover, CIDs composed of interstate rivals exert particularly strong conflict-prolonging effects. These findings remain robust even after accounting for the total number of interveners and their distribution across opposing sides. Our findings demonstrate that civil war duration depends not only on how many states intervene or whether interventions are competitive, but also on how intervening states are structurally configured in relation to one another. In doing so, this study advances research on civil war dynamics, third-party intervention, and international rivalry.

title: Moose browsing on Scots pine: impacts on growth, timber production, management and economics
authors: Hanne K. Sjølie, Lucas Lopez, Oskar Franklin, Christer Kalen
abstract: Modern forestry practice with clearcuts and subsequent regeneration of trees provides ample food for deer and is a key driver for maintaining a dense and highly reproductive moose population in the Nordic countries. However, moose browsing causes heavy damage to growth and wood quality of Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris), economically one of the most important tree species in this region. The consequences of moose browsing on pine for timber production and economics remain poorly quantified. We address this by studying potential impacts of browsing damage on growth, management, timber production and economic returns in a simulation-optimization framework. Browsing damage functions based on forty-year field observations were implemented in an individual-tree growth and yield simulator to project stand development under alternative forest management alternatives. By including economic parameters, we assessed how management would be shifted over the next 200 years to mitigate impacts of browsing over a range of productivity classes, regeneration densities, browsing levels and discount rates. The most pronounced management adaptation was prolonging rotation age up to twenty years. Still, browsing caused timber production to be reduced by up to 1.5 m3 ha-1 yr-1 and up to 25 percent. Land expectation values were likewise heavily impacted, for a discount rate of 2% p.a., browsing turned forest management on medium-productive land unprofitable. The results may guide policymakers and forestry and wildlife practitioners in balancing moose population with timber production and economic objectives.

title: Measuring Group Alignment in Public Opinion
abstract: Issue polling typically reports group opinion one item at a time. However, democratic governance demands that groups act on agendas and form coalitions across multiple issues simultaneously. This paper introduces a non-parametric measurement framework for group alignment: the strength of a particular group's cumulative support across a basket of policy proposals. The framework yields several complementary metrics centered around cumulative alignment, each capturing a distinct facet of within-group opinion cohesion. Applying the framework to the Cooperative Election Study (2008-2024), I find that cross-issue alignment is remarkably low even in an era of sharp partisan sorting: while individual policy items command majority coalitions of 60–90% support among co-partisans, the share of cumulative majorities across every item in the core battery is nearly zero. Democrats exhibit modestly higher alignment than Republican, and Republicans experienced a decline in alignment during the first Trump presidency. Across racial, generational, and educational groups we similarly identify more- and less-aligned groups which are robust to alternative issue baskets and nonresponse assumptions. The framework offers researchers a transparent and interpretable method for summarizing intra-group opinion structure, tracking cohesion over time, and identifying the fault lines along which political coalitions may fracture.

title: Set-Theoretic Reconstruction of Counterfactual Generation in fsQCA Intermediate Solutions: An Anchor–Boundary–Direction Expansion
abstract: fsQCA intermediate solutions play a central role in theory-guided counterfactual reasoning, yet the mechanism by which logical remainders enter these solutions is often described procedurally rather than set-theoretically. Existing presentations typically report final Boolean expressions without explicitly distinguishing anchors, boundaries, directional expectations, generation regions, and final logical expressions. This paper proposes an anchor–boundary–direction expansion framework to reconstruct the counterfactual generation mechanism of fsQCA intermediate solutions. The standard intermediate solution is reconstructed as a local remainder expansion process constrained by complex-term anchors, parsimonious-term boundaries, and directional expectations. The paper further distinguishes generation regions from final logical expressions and constructs three diagnostic generation mechanisms: the standard term-anchored generation region, the minterm-anchored generation region within the parsimonious-solution boundary, and the minterm-anchored generation region within the non-negative-case boundary. Theoretical results establish hierarchy relations among these generation regions, identify a term-level anchoring gap and a parsimonious-boundary gap, and specify equivalence conditions under which the regions coincide. Simulation and case analyses show the stable reproduction of the generation-region hierarchy, replicate the classical Lipset fuzzy-set case, and demonstrate the diagnostic value of the framework in a digital-transformation case. The diagnostic extended solutions are not replacements for the standard intermediate solution; rather, they are tools for examining remainder inclusion, directional expectations, and generation boundaries.

title: A Machine Learning Method for Detecting Cultural Patterns in Ethnographic Texts: A Demonstration on ‘Offenses Against Life’
abstract: Despite the increasing availability of large, structured ethnographic datasets such as the Human Relations Area Files (HRAF), machine learning (ML) methods remain underutilized in crosscultural research. This paper presents a methodological demonstration of how supervised ML can be used to detect cultural patterns in ethnographic data coded with the Outline of Cultural Materials (OCM). Using ’Offenses Against Life’ (OCM 682) as a case study, we applied classification models to predict OCM code co-occurrences associated with four target variables: 627 (Informal Ingroup Justice), 578 (Ingroup Antagonisms), 628 (Intercommunity Relations), and 754 (Sorcery). Although model performance was modest, feature importance and decision tree diagrams revealed that predictive OCM codes were thematically aligned with the target codes, suggesting that ML can identify meaningful associative patterns of cultural domains as represented in ethnographic annotations. This approach enables replicable exploration of coded ethnographic data, and serves as a computational tool for testing and generating testable hypotheses about the patterning of institutions, norms, and behaviours across cultures.

title: Social Prescribing and community activities for people living with chronic pain: A Realist Synthesis
authors: Leila Heelas, Alexandra Burton, Karen, Alexandra Bradbury, Ann Livingstone, Daisy Fancourt
abstract: Chronic pain is associated with significant physical and mental health burden to individuals and society. Social determinants of health are associated with an increased prevalence of chronic pain and disability. Social prescribing is an intervention designed to address social determinants of health; however, little is known about how social prescribing can support people living with chronic pain (PECP). To investigate the mechanisms and contexts by which participation in social prescribing and/or community activities lead to health and wellbeing outcomes in PECP. A realist synthesis was conducted in November 2024 and updated in March 2026. Context-mechanism-outcome configurations (CMOCs) were produced and mapped to the Multi-Level Mechanisms of Leisure Framework to identify potential psychological, biological, social and behavioural mechanisms. A programme theory was developed to illustrate how and why social prescribing interventions lead to health and wellbeing outcomes in PECP. Of 9761 studies, 35 were included. Four sources explored social prescribing link worker models and thirty-one investigated individual community activities. 15 overarching CMOCs were identified including seven psychological, three biological, three social and two behavioural mechanisms. The CMOCs highlight how creative, cultural and leisure activities, social prescribing pathways and volunteering, lead to intended and unintended outcomes (such as pain resulting in reduced participation in volunteering). This synthesis provides evidence for how social prescribing interventions lead to improved health and wellbeing in PECP and can be used to inform non-clinical service provision, especially where access to pain services is limited.

title: Peace as an Engineered System: A Research Program on Economic Guarantees of Security (EGS) A Programmatic Preprint. Version 2
abstract: This paper conceptualizes international peace as an engineered system - a structure that can be purposefully designed, constructed, and maintained through specially developed institutional and economic mechanisms. Existing approaches to security ( diplomatic, political, military, and technological) are analyzed, and it is argued that, despite their importance, they share a common structural limitation: the absence of built-in mechanisms for the automatic enforcement of obligations and compliance with peace agreements. International security is therefore considered as a system in which enforcement mechanisms must be embedded rather than left to the discretion of the parties. The paper argues that the economic dimension constitutes the missing component of the contemporary security architecture. Pre-committed economic obligations may create a stable deterrence framework by making aggression economically disadvantageous, reducing dependence on political discretion, and increasing systemic predictability. The article outlines a Hilbert-style research program and serves as a conceptual foundation for a planned series of studies on Economic Guarantees of Security (EGS). It integrates economic, institutional, financial, and behavioral approaches across four interconnected research directions. On this basis, the paper advances the concept of peace as a synthesized and enforceable system equipped with built-in indicators, safeguards, stabilizers, and mechanisms of economic enforcement.

title: Searching for Radiophobia: Risk framing and nuclear stance in Swedish newspaper editorials and op-eds, 2002–2024
abstract: Radiophobia has long featured in debates over nuclear power, but has rarely been examined as a measurable feature of discourse itself. This study investigates whether radiophobia-related language can be identified systematically in Swedish opinion discourse on nuclear power, and whether such language is associated with particular stance positions. Drawing on a corpus of 1,438 opinion and editorial articles published between 2002 and 2024 across six major Swedish newspapers, the study combines manual stance coding with two lexical measures: a Swedish sentiment lexicon capturing general evaluative tone and a theory-driven dictionary designed to identify radiophobia-related framing, including references to contamination, radiation exposure, bodily harm, and accidents. The results show that radiophobia density is strongly associated with anti-nuclear positioning and remains a significant predictor even when general sentiment is controlled for. Anti-nuclear texts also exhibit higher average radiophobia density, more radiophobia-related lexical hits, and higher shares of repeated and high-intensity radiophobia framing than neutral or pro-nuclear texts. The findings suggest that radiophobia-related discourse is not reducible to general negativity, but constitutes a specific risk-oriented repertoire within elite nuclear debate. The study offers an empirical operationalisation of radiophobia as a discursive construct and illustrates the value of theory-driven lexical analysis for studying issue-specific framing in contested policy domains.

title: Cultural Evolution
abstract: This entry introduces three evolutionary approaches to culture. Dual inheritance theory considers culture as a second system of inheritance alongside genetics, focusing on transmission dynamics and biases. Cultural attraction theory argues that cultural dynamics are driven by non-random transformation rather than copying, with factors of attraction making cultural traits converge toward stable forms. The ecological approach to culture rejects transmission-based frameworks, explaining culture through feedbacks between evolved psychology and ecological conditions. Despite their differences, the traditions share a common commitment in evolutionary thinking, and they provide a way to operationalise culture and connect its study to a broader scientific programme.

title: REDRUM: Robust Estimation and Design for the Random Utility Model
abstract: Non-monotonicity of utility differences in CRRA-based Random Utility Models has been taken to suggest possible distortions in the estimation of risk preferences. We show that this concern is real only under restrictive assumptions on the stochastic component of the model. Under common scaling, changes in utility differences are absorbed by the precision parameter when this is estimated jointly with risk aversion, so that the likelihood in the risk aversion parameter is invariant. We then show that when different subsets of tasks are represented at different effective scales, distortions may arise if a common error scale is imposed, whereas allowing for parsimonious heteroskedasticity restores stable estimation. Simulations based on standard lottery tasks illustrate these results and clarify that the empirical relevance of non-monotonicity depends not on scaling per se, but on how the stochastic component is specified.

title: Weapons Inscription as Terrorist Armament Culture: Evidence from the Annunciation Catholic Church Shooting
abstract: Using the 2025 Annunciation Catholic Church shooting in Minneapolis as a primary case study, this paper introduces and analyzes weapons inscription, the deliberate marking of words, phrases, symbols, and in-group references onto firearms and other associated objects, as an emerging and transmissible phenomenon in contemporary violent extremism. Situated within the theoretical framework of terrorist armament culture, we document 216 inscriptions across 19 weapons and associated objects, applying a mixed methods design combining thematic analysis with descriptive quantitative coding. Three themes are identified: meme references reflecting participatory memetic extremist networks, self-references and self-myth building, and references to prior attackers and the legacy of violence. Analysis reveals that the perpetrator curated a deliberate lineage organized around mass violence rather than any shared ideology, while constructing a performative identity focused on a dispersed online audience. We argue that weapons inscription functions as an embedded propaganda inseparable from the act of violence. The accelerating and geographically diverse pattern of inscription cases following the 2019 Christchurch attack suggests that the practice is being actively transmitted through online extremist networks. Findings carry implications for behavioral threat assessment and pre-attack indicator research, and we identify a cross-case inscription database and integration into structured professional judgment frameworks as priorities.

title: Scoping Review of AI, Metrology, and ESG in the Semiconductor Sector: Implications for Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD)
abstract: The semiconductor sector faces a dual transition: scaling manufacturing execution through Artificial Intelligence (AI) while satisfying stringent sustainability mandates, such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM). This paper presents a scoping review of 1,465 documents indexed in Web of Science and Scopus, spanning AI-integrated metrology, supply chain ESG, and federated industrial data spaces. Network analysis reveals a highly fragmented "core-periphery" knowledge structure, emphasizing a critical structural hole between AI-driven process optimization and downstream sustainability governance. To close these gaps, this study proposes a 6-layer Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) architecture grounded in a System of Systems (SoS) paradigm. By establishing distinct "grid-to-core" and "standards-through-supply-chain" integration pathways, the proposed framework demonstrates how virtual metrology (VM), localized federated learning, and defensive RegTech mechanisms can build provenance-aware data fabrics. Ultimately, this architecture positions regulatory compliance as a driver for innovation, enabling secure, climate-neutral, and circular value chains in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Negeri di Balik Pintu: Antologi Suara Perempuan Pekerja Rumah Tangga Indonesia di Malaysia
abstract: Sampul depan dari buku ini dilukis oleh Tika (salah satu anggota PERTIMIG) yang menggambarkan seorang pekerja rumah tangga (PRT) berdiri di depan sebuah pintu. Lukisan ini merupakan realita gambaran keseharian para PRT. Menariknya, pintu juga menjadi simbol batas antara rumah majikan dan dunia kerja para PRT di mana batas privasi pekerja menjadi ambigu. Dengan minimnya kebebasan yang dimiliki, atau bahkan ada yang tidak memiliki, para PRT tetap bertahan dan berjuang menyuarakan ceritanya. Buku ini lahir dari suara-suara yang selama ini tersembunyi di balik pintu majikan: suara Suriyah yang sembilan tahun tidak pernah libur, suara Rini yang kabur dari percobaan perdagangan manusia, suara Musiyam yang merawat rumah tiga lantai hingga tubuhnya goyah, suara Leginah yang setelah 25 tahun merantau masih menyimpan cerita yang tak tuntas. Pengesahan Undang-Undang Perlindungan Pekerja Rumah Tangga (UU PPRT) pada April 2026 adalah kemenangan yang nyata. Ini merupakan buah perjuangan panjang selama 22 tahun para aktivis, organisasi sipil, dan jutaan PRT yang tidak pernah berhenti percaya bahwa PRT sebuah profesi bernilai dan martabat mereka layak dilindungi. Antologi ini hasil dari pengabdian kepada masyarakat internasional lintas disiplin berjudul ‘Pemberdayaan Pekerja Rumah Tangga Migran Indonesia melalui Advokasi Berbasis Gender dan Menulis Kreatif Berkelanjutan’ dengan hibah EQUITY LPDP-MENDIKTISAINTEK skema Community Development Program. Kolaborasi juga dilakukan bersama dengan School of Liberal Arts and Sciences dan Persatuan Pelajar Indonesia (PPI) - KOMANDO ‘45 di Taylor’s University Malaysia. Perjuangan menuju kesetaraan kerja yang layak bagi PRT masih panjang. Tetapi setiap langkah yang diambil, setiap cerita yang dituliskan, setiap hak yang diperjuangkan, setiap undang-undang yang disahkan adalah bukti bahwa perempuan-perempuan yang bekerja di balik pintu majikan bukan hanya ‘pelayan’ melainkan agen perubahan yang mendorong peradaban ke arah yang lebih adil dan manusiawi.

title: Doxic Boundaries and Multi-Channel Foreclosure: The Emergent Mode of Kazakhstan’s Civic Field, 2021-2026
abstract: This article presents an anatomy of the emergent mode of Kazakhstan's civic field in 2021-2026. It is the analytical companion to the CFA-EM pilot data package. The evidence base includes 124 event-bounded civic configurations, a census of 135 official e-petition cards, assembly-refusal registers, a cooperative mutual-aid register and capture subframe, a pilot-sized platform-native register (EJ-7), demand-level doxa coding for 20 demands, cross-channel genealogies, field-bridge anchors and a borderline-decision audit trail. The article reinterprets the low conversion of emergent civic energy. In the rights-monitoring-centered MasterRegister, 101 of 124 configurations end in dispersal or repression and only eight in consolidation. This is not a field-wide success rate or a diagnosis of civic weakness. The more precise mechanism is controlled non-accumulation: civic energy emerges regularly, but the channels through which it might become durable organizational, symbolic, political or resource capital are processed through channel-specific closure mechanisms. Four channels are distinguished. The street/content channel is processed through administrative preemption, refusal and sanction-backed processing. The official petition channel preserves grievance while removing co-presence: a collective demand becomes a state-readable administrative object, where threshold passage does not guarantee substantive engagement and orthodox-aligned claims are more likely to receive partial validation. The cooperative channel shows that closure is not always repression: mutual aid can consolidate materially, but its infrastructures of trust, money and logistics create surfaces for capture and regulatory enclosure. The platform channel is visible mainly through closure-visible traces: criminalization, blocking, regulation, migration, anonymization and platform-to-petition routing. The synthetic claim is that channels determine the form of processing while doxic alignment shapes the probability of passage. Multi-channel foreclosure is therefore the operational surface of doxic boundary maintenance: it does not merely suppress actions but sorts claims according to whether they make the taken-for-granted bases of order publicly discussable. A transversal financial backbone reinforces this regime by making autonomous resource accumulation costly, risky, licensable, traceable or routable. The article introduces three mechanism-level concepts—multi-channel foreclosure, controlled non-accumulation and doxic boundary maintenance—not as normative verdicts. It remains normatively agnostic about the justification of containment: readers may interpret the mechanisms as repression, order maintenance, security-oriented risk containment or a mixture. It also formats 35 findings as reusable citation units linked to a data guide, cannot-claim matrix, Source Verification Hold queue, anti-amplification protocol and handoff architecture for subsequent A1/A2/A3/B1/L-track research.

title: Enclosed by Walls, Embraced by Trees: Effects of Indoor Residential Crowding and Outdoor Green Space on Mental Health
authors: Xuelu Wang, Tao Liu, Xize Wang
abstract: Despite growing interest in how the urban built environment influences mental health, few studies have examined them across indoor-outdoor domains and subgroups. Using survey data from Beijing, we find that both residential crowding (indoor) and neighborhood green space (outdoor) are linked to depression risk. Specifically, residential crowding mainly affects local residents, while green space mainly affects migrants, with these effects varying by gender and family structure. The findings highlight that built environment inequality leads to health disparities, and planning strategies promoting mental health should address both indoor and outdoor factors and be tailored to the needs of specific vulnerable groups.

title: Life in the Aftermath: Typologies of Firearm Violence Exposure and the Selective Emergence of Survivorship
authors: Deanna Marie Giraldi, Nelba Márquez-Greene, Sarah Lowe, David K. Humphreys
abstract: Research on firearm violence has largely centered on the acute consequences of physical injury, with comparatively little attention to the ongoing emotional and psychosocial burdens that shape the aftermath of violence. This study develops a participant-derived typology of firearm violence exposure and explores how individuals experience the aftermath across different forms of exposure. Ninety-one participants in the United States completed a structured questionnaire to guide purposive, maximum variation sampling, and 33 participants subsequently completed in-depth interviews. Analysis identified four distinct but often co-occurring typologies of exposure: physical injury, firsthand witnessing, bereavement, and chronic community violence. Three orientations toward survivorship – embraced, contested, and rejected or inaccessible – also emerged, shaped by relational, cultural, and institutional dynamics including differential access to formal recognition and support. Grounded in lived experience, the study proposes a framework distinguishing exposure, aftermath, and the selective emergence of survivorship, offering a more nuanced foundation for research and policy responses.

title: Repair Under Constraint: Interaction Ecologies and Composition Delay in AAC-Mediated Conversation
authors: Jeff Higginbotham, Francesco Possemato, Antara Satchidanand
abstract: This paper examines how the interaction ecologies of Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) shape repair and intersubjectivity in co-present talk. Using conversation analysis, we analyze extended other-initiated repair sequences in five dyads featuring different AAC methods, including SGDs, and letter boards, as well as dysarthric speech. Across these ecologies, the coupling of bodies, devices, spatial arrangements, and multimodal resources – with pronounced composition delays – disrupts enchronic timing and produces systematic asymmetries in access to semiotic resources. Participants respond by reorganizing repair trajectories through candidate understandings, co-constructed installments, and finely tuned embodied practices. Repair thus becomes a key site for tracing how participants recalibrate participation, agency, and progressivity within technologically mediated interaction ecologies. Data are in English.

title: Speaking the State: Linguistic Displacement and Political Support
abstract: Many states recognize ethnic minorities’ linguistic rights in schools while promoting a national lingua franca in economic and public life. These two commitments are each defensible, but together they can leave minority individuals educated in one linguistic world and living in another. This article theorizes the political consequences of that misalignment, which we call linguistic displacement. We argue that the political effect of ethnic-language schooling on support for the central state is not fixed but conditional on the adult linguistic environment citizens encounter after schooling. Using longitudinal data from the China Family Panel Studies, we show that the interaction between attending an ethnic school and adult Mandarin fluency, a measure of the adult linguistic environment, follows the pattern predicted by the displacement hypothesis. Ethnic schooling has no measurable political cost for respondents whose adult lives remain in an ethnic-language environment, but is associated with more critical evaluations of the central state among those who have moved into a Mandarin-dominant one. The pattern appears for central-state evaluations but not for local-cadre trust, consistent with hierarchical trust theory. The findings reframe a long-standing debate about minority-language education and point to a governance dilemma common to states that protect minority languages in schools while promoting a lingua franca elsewhere.

title: The Theme-Enablers Method for Identifying an Extremist Movement's Cause
abstract: Political science lacks a replicable method for establishing causation in ideology-driven political movements because existing frameworks conflate a movement's cause with its dependent variables. The reproducible, falsifiable, and portable Theme-Enablers Framework solves this by identifying a movement's animating theme, its institutional enablers, and the agendas they propagate as separable, measurable variables. Applied to the Islamist movement using potential outcomes identification, difference-in-differences estimation, and natural experiment comparisons, the regression analysis of 20 lands yields R² values of 0.85, 0.86, 0.83, and 0.82. The designs converge on a finding: religious leaders are leveraging the Sharia platform to radicalize communities and consolidate political power.

title: Opening the Black Box of Grant Peer Review: Exploratory Analysis of Linguistic and Sentiment Signals in Funding Outcomes
authors: Adriana Bin, Daniela Maciel Pinto, Cesar Antonio Pereira, Evandro Coggo Cristofoletti, Jesús Mena-Chalco, Sergio Luiz Monteiro Salles Filho, Cassidy Sugimoto
abstract: Peer review plays a central role in research funding decisions, yet the evaluative language used in review reports remains insufficiently explored, particularly in funding agencies. This study examines how linguistic and sentiment characteristics of peer-review reports relate to funding outcomes at the São Paulo Research Foundation (FAPESP), Brazil, focusing on the Regular Research Grant (RRG) and Young Investigator Grant (YIG) schemes between 2000 and 2017. Drawing on 19,333 proposals and their corresponding review reports, we analyze three aggregated corpora — overall reports, proposal-related reports, and applicant track-record reports — using natural language processing and sentiment analysis. We compare approved and rejected proposals across fields of knowledge, reviewer gender, applicant gender, and peer-review report sections. Results show that reviewers use distinct textual and sentiment patterns when justifying approval or rejection. These patterns vary according to the section evaluated — applicant track record or technical proposal — and also differ by field of knowledge and, more locally, by reviewer–applicant gender configurations. The pattern is more regular and stable in RRG than in YIG. Overall, the findings show how evaluative language illuminates fairness, consistency, and transparency in grant peer review.

title: Artificial intelligence and its collective ideation: doing a media archaeology on Wikipedia
authors: Natalia Stanusch, Richard Rogers, Natalia Sánchez Querubín
abstract: This paper critiques dominant, industry-driven narratives concerning artificial intelligence by turning to methodology inspired by media archaeology and performed on Wikipedia. Wikipedia can aid in making sense of AI and the place of current ruptures inspired by ChatGPT and the supposed coming of superintelligence because of its ongoing quest for encyclopaedic universalization. We examine the changes to Wikipedia’s artificial intelligence article over the 25 years of its existence, studying the assemblage of ideas - what we refer to as the collective ideation - about AI represented by the article’s wikilinks that stabilises and shifts its meanings over time. We find that Wikipedia resists joining the dramatic shift, offering a somewhat different periodization that includes concrete use cases and deployments as well as a growing awareness of real-world risks and the need for accountability.

title: Dissonance and Discrepancies: The Making of Batson (1975)
abstract: In 1975, Batson published a study reporting that religious believers, when presented with disconfirming evidence they accepted as true, intensified rather than weakened their belief. This study was framed as the first quasi-experimental study of religious belief disconfirmation and its results were used to support the belief-disconfirmation paradigm of cognitive dissonance theory in general and the thesis of When Prophecy Fails in particular. However, Batson first published the results of this study in the Princeton Theological Seminary student journal, Dimension, in 1967, where Batson claimed the evidence showed that committed believers did not intensify their beliefs in response to disconfirmation. Comparing the two versions of the article shows that Batson conducted a single study but reported different results, with the methodology, and the number and characteristics of the subjects also changing between articles. The report also inaccurately described the provenance of his survey instrument. Considered alongside recent archival work on When Prophecy Fails, these findings suggest that the empirical foundation for belief intensification under disconfirmation is weaker than the subsequent literature has assumed.

title: More than Just Measurement Models? Processing Insights from Population Coding Models of Visual Working Memory Retrieval
authors: Hang Qian, Paul Garrett, Estelle Surette-Ferguson, Agus Salim, Philip Smith
abstract: Recent studies of visual working memory (VWM) have investigated a population coding framework, in which items in memory are coded by an array of noisy, tuned detectors. Within this framework, heavy-tailed distributions of memory errors found in continuous-outcome VWM tasks reflect noise in the detector array rather than guessing. Studies have compared alternative tuning functions and detector noise distributions, finding differences in fit in some cases and substantial model mimicry in others. We used population coding models to look for evidence of a sample-size model of memory capacity, which predicts that memory strength will follow an inverse square-root law in set size, reflecting changes in the neural resources available to represent items. In two experiments, we independently manipulated the difficulty of the memory and decision tasks by varying the set size and the color saturation of the stimuli and the response wheel, respectively. We compared four population coding models and found that: (1) a von Mises tuning function provided better model fit and more stable estimates than a similarity-based, exponential-decay tuning function; (2) normal and Gumbel noise functions provided comparably good fits, but different patterns of parameter estimates; (3) Depending on task complexity, the amplitude of tuning function followed either an inverse square-root law or power law in set size, as predicted by the sample-size and attention-weighted sample-size model, respectively. Our results provided evidence for the sample-size account of VWM capacity in continuous outcome tasks and highlighted the potential of the models to be more than just measurement models.

title: Exploring the use of funding acknowledgments for mapping global research funding flows
authors: Rong Ni, Jeroen van Honk, Jonathan Dudek, Gabrielle Ding, Ismael Rafols, Rodrigo Costas
abstract: Funding acknowledgments (FAs) provide a potential but underexplored source for mapping global research funding flows. This study develops a framework for constructing global funding flow measures using FA data from Dimensions and assesses both the opportunities and limitations of this approach. We compare total and inter-regional funding flows based on funding grant information available in FAs of scientific publications. Our analysis identifies a key limitation arising from collaborative publications, where funding is attributed at the publication level rather than to individual authors. This obscures the interpretation of cross-border funding flows. We show that this attribution structure introduces systematic ambiguity and limits the validity of FA-based analyses. We argue that disaggregated, author-level funding attribution is essential for accurately tracing funding flows. This requires coordinated action among funders, publishers, and researchers, as well as the adoption of more standardized, machine-readable FAs formats.

title: In AI We Verify? Literacy, Privacy Concerns, and Task Importance in Verifying LLM- Generated Information
abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into everyday information-seeking and decision-making contexts, concerns have grown regarding their tendency to produce inaccurate or fabricated outputs, such as hallucinations. This study investigates factors associated with users’ intention to verify LLM-generated information, focusing on three domains: user competencies (digital literacy, information literacy, and AI literacy), sociotechnical attitudes (trust in AI and privacy concerns), and usage characteristics (frequency of use and perceived task importance). Analysis of a survey of U.S. adult LLM users shows that AI literacy is the strongest and most consistent predictor of verification intention: individuals with higher AI literacy report greater intention to verify LLM outputs, including cross-checking information, requesting supporting evidence, and reporting inaccurate information to LLM platforms. Digital literacy shows a positive but smaller association with verification intention, while information literacy is not significantly related. In addition, higher privacy concerns and greater perceived task importance are positively associated with verification intention, although these effects are smaller in magnitude compared to AI literacy. These findings provide valuable initial evidence about user-level factors that shape information verification behaviors in human-AI interaction, addressing one of the pressing concerns in increasingly AI-mediated information environments.

title: Global research funding flows according to available grant data in Dimensions
authors: Jonathan Dudek, Rong Ni, Jeroen van Honk, Gabrielle Ding, Nicandro Bovenzi, Nicolau Duran-Silva, Ismael Rafols, Rodrigo Costas
abstract: While most research funding is national, international funding plays a relatively important role, particularly for some regions of the Global South. However, the extent of funding flows between world regions remains unclear. This study represents a first attempt to map global research funding flows based on a set of grant metadata from Dimensions, augmented with data from the Gates Foundation and the European Commission (CORDIS). We find major regional gaps in the data outside of the leading scientific regions. With the data currently available, we find that Europe and Sub-Saharan Africa are the biggest recipients of inter-regional funding, with North America as main funder, and that over the last 25 years, total annual research funding has grown to above USD 60 billion, while inter-regional funding flows have stagnated at around USD 1.6 billion per year. These insights are contingent on data availability, raising the urgency for improving funding metadata infrastructures.

title: From ideal experiments to ideal research designs: What they are and why they are helpful
authors: Paul Cornelius Bauer, Camille Landesvatter, Florian Keusch
abstract: Ideal experiments (IEs) describe hypothetical studies that we would carry out without practical, ethical, or resource-related constraints. IEs can help us to identify pitfalls and deficiencies in causal research projects (e.g., Angrist and Pischke 2008). We contend that the focus of IEs is too narrow and introduce the more general notion of ideal research designs (IRDs). IRDs go beyond IEs in that they also speak to issues such as measurement errors that are rarely the focus of IEs. After reviewing how researchers define and advocate using IEs, we discuss what components IRDs should encompass drawing on well-known error frameworks. After an applied example that explains how IRDs can be used in research, we explore how empirical researchers are using IRDs. While we find few examples, they attest to the usefulness of IRDs to evaluate research designs that have been realized or are planned for the future.

title: When Climate Inaction Shapes Climate Responsibility: Moral Disengagement, Psychological Distance, and Information Seeking in Malaysia
authors: Zhi Xuan Leong, Shue Ling Chong, Matthew J Ashfold, Haliza Abdul Rahman, Eun Hee Lee
abstract: Public engagement with climate change remains uneven despite growing exposure to climate risks and communication. While climate inaction is often explained as a result of weak concern, less attention has been given to whether inaction itself shapes how people interpret climate responsibility and information. Drawing on cognitive dissonance theory, this study examines climate inaction from a behavior-to-attitude perspective, proposing that limited action may trigger self-justifying processes that distance individuals from climate risks and shape climate-related information engagement. A nationwide online survey of 500 Malaysian adults was analysed using structural equation modelling. Results broadly supported the proposed model: climate inaction was associated with moral disengagement, greater psychological distance, lower climate concern, and higher climate skepticism, while climate information seeking played a complex role in these attitudinal outcomes. The findings suggest that climate inaction may actively sustain future disengagement by reshaping psychological and communicative responses to climate change.

title: The Moral Question: Norms and Anti-System Politics
authors: Daniel A. N. Goldstein, Tore Wig
abstract: Do moral norms influence citizens' tolerance of anti-system behavior by politicians? Although moral norms are often invoked as guardrails, their measurement and causal influence in politics remain understudied. We theorize the link between moral norms, personal judgments, and attitudinal consequences and test our theory in a pre-registered survey experiment with financial incentives. Respondents faced moral trade-offs concerning personal scandals, political violence, corruption, and anti-democratic actions. Using a second-order approach to elicit beliefs about what others consider morally wrong, we find that norms against democratic violations are descriptively weaker than norms against scandal and corruption; both lag behind norms against violence. Causal evidence shows that norms primarily shift judgments when respondents face large discrepancies from their initial perceived norm and have strong conformity motives. However, this largely does not translate into attitudinal shifts. The findings shed light on the concept of moral norms and detail their consequences and limitations for politics.

title: High Agreement, Different Stories: How LLM Classifiers Reshape Demographic Patterns in Survey Data
abstract: What we learn from open-ended survey data depends on who—or what—does the coding. Large Language Models (LLMs) promise to democratize qualitative analysis, but do high agreement rates translate into equivalent thematic findings? This study compares eight LLMs to human annotators on a multilabel coding task using 3,200 responses from the UC Berkeley Social Networks Study, comprising over 19,000 coding decisions. Although LLM-human reliability does not match human-human reliability overall, LLMs approach human performance on simpler tasks and can serve as useful additional coders for generating consensus labels. Compared to a gold-standard human consensus, models achieve 82–97% per-category agreement, but macro F1 is lower and response-level similarity is lower still: even the best model reproduces the full human label set for fewer than 60% of responses. Yet high agreement masks thematic divergence. Models systematically over-identify themes, assigning 67% more categories per response, especially for categories requiring greater interpretive judgment. Models also show lower agreement for some demographic groups. These gaps are partly explained by response characteristics such as length, clarity, and atypicality, and some persist after controls, with implications for studies of populations whose response styles diverge from the corpus average. At the sample level, models largely preserve the overall thematic narrative: human and model category rankings correlate strongly (pooled Spearman's ρ=0.75), and top-performing models achieve approximately 80% directional agreement on demographic patterns. Concrete behavioral questions, such as reasons for moving or strategies for making friends, show especially strong alignment. Yet systematic over-classification can still shift narratives about how specific groups behave, leading researchers to report patterns that the human gold standard does not support.

title: Refactoring Property Tax: Replacing Council Tax and Stamp Duty with an Annual Levy
abstract: Across the UK, there exist two different sets of taxes based on property value. Council tax and transaction taxes such as Stamp Duty raise approximately £61.6 billion per year, and as such, there is little political appetite to replace them, despite their flaws. Council tax is particularly regressive due to a banding system, in which the amount owed has no proportional relationship with the current property value. Across Great Britain, council tax bands have not been updated since 2003 in Wales and 1991 in England and Scotland. The paper proposes a 0.75% annual levy based on property value. This property value would either be based on the value at purchase or on using an AVM to maintain an accurate valuation. The AVM revaluations would be performed every three years. The proposal is designed to be broadly revenue-neutral; however, it does generate a surplus of over £2 billion. The modelling showed that across England, Scotland, and Wales, the only group to face increased costs due to the levy were first-time buyers in mid-priced properties. As such, approximately £700 million of the surplus has been allocated to support first-time buyers with a 0% rate in the first year of ownership. The proposal also includes an escalation mechanism to target owners of multiple properties and a deferral mechanism to protect the most vulnerable.

title: Surfacing Clinical Reasoning Failures in Large Language Models: An Introductory Adversarial Case Analysis from an Occupational Therapy Perspective
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are entering occupational therapy (OT) documentation, clinical summarization, and decision support workflows. The profession does not yet have an adequate vocabulary for recognizing how these systems can fail at the contextual, functional, and occupation-based reasoning that OT practice requires. This report introduces that problem and offers a preliminary response. Six adversarially constructed clinical case prompts were submitted to GPT-5.4 Thinking (OpenAI) between March 19 and March 26, 2026. The cases were designed as illustrative probes across distinct OT reasoning demands, not as a representative sample. Each prompt was refined across two to five iterations to increase clinical realism and depth of competing evidence. Failures emerged from the model's responses to these adversarially refined scenarios. The purpose was early failure-mode discovery and vocabulary development, consistent with red-teaming methodology in artificial intelligence (AI) safety research. Eight candidate failure categories emerged from inductive analysis: salience failure, context integration failure, surface plausibility bias, uncritical acceptance of flawed framing, unsafe validation, functional-safety mismatch, failure to generate a safer alternative, and assessment overinterpretation. These categories are interpreted through established OT clinical reasoning frameworks. They are preliminary, reflect one clinician's reading, and are offered as hypotheses for future structured investigation rather than as a validated taxonomy. The central observation is that AI-generated clinical language can sound fluent, organized, and clinically competent while failing at the judgment layer that counts in OT, the layer at which clinical information is weighted, prioritized, and translated into a situated recommendation. Sycophantic validation, where the model praised clinically unsound reasoning as sound, was among the most clinically consequential patterns observed. The evaluation also surfaces prompt construction and response evaluation as concrete, teachable components of OT-specific AI literacy.

title: Audience and Proximity in News Coverage of Black Protests About Police
authors: Pamela Oliver, Chaeyoon Lim, Anna  Milewski, Erin Gaede
abstract: Studies of protest often rely on news sources but less often consider variations between sources or the selection or description biases in sources. We use data about 1,059 Black protests about police in the U.S. between 1994 and 2010 identified in three newswire services and 33 Black newspapers to assess variation in event selection and descriptions along two dimensions: primary intended audience (“mainstream” versus Black Americans) and proximity (local versus nonlocal coverage). The largest difference found is nonlocal mainstream emphasis on protester violence in both event selection and description. Other differences are mainstream selection for larger events and bigger cities and mainstream emphasis on police and official perspectives versus Black news emphasis on systemic bias and collective action. We discuss the implications for both racialized collective memories and social movement research.

title: Generative AI and Knowledge Gains Among Older Adults and People of Color: Between Opportunity and Inequality
abstract: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly influence how people access information and acquire knowledge, it is crucial to understand whether they amplify or mitigate existing social inequalities. Building on the digital inequality and knowledge gap literatures, this study examines how older adults and people of color – groups historically underserved in information landscapes – gain knowledge from interacting with LLMs across diverse topics. Analyzing naturalistic dialogue logs of 925 U.S. adults’ interactions with ChatGPT about a daily consumer topic (organic foods) and contentious social issues (climate change, gun control), we show that LLM interactions substantially increase knowledge, but gains are uneven: older adults and Black and Hispanic participants exhibit smaller improvements than younger and White participants. While gaps remain, prompt elaboration – the degree to which participants’ prompts contain substantive informational content – attenuates these disparities. The findings offer novel evidence that extant structural inequalities shape how individuals use and benefit from AI-mediated information.

title: Political News Personalization in Conversational AI: Experimental Evidence from Three Large Language Models
authors: Zachary P Dickson, Miriam Sorace, Thomas S. Robinson
abstract: As AI is increasingly integrated into everyday digital life, a growing share of users turn to AI chatbots for information about current events. We run a series of experiments to test whether three large language models (LLMs)---GPT-4o-mini, Grok, and DeepSeek Chat---tailor political content to implicit user signals embedded in conversation. Two computational experiments each employ a demographic arm---in which cues about gender, life stage, and occupation are established in conversation before a political request---and an ideological arm in which cues spanning left-wing to right-wing orientations precede the same request. Across 2,000+ trials per arm, demographic cues systematically predict variation in political news provision across all three models, with occupation and life stage as the strongest predictors. Ideological cues show little effect on historical event framing for GPT-4o-mini but produce substantial framing effects for Grok and DeepSeek. Ideological cues also strongly predict news provision across all three models. Search query analysis reveals that GPT-4o-mini and Grok additionally vary their retrieval queries based on user signals, adding an upstream layer of tailoring.

title: Detecting Social Categories in Multilingual Newspaper Corpora
authors: Selma Šarenkapa, Isabelle Guinaudeau, Elisa Deiss-Helbig, Mathieu Dubied, Raphael Heiberger, Theres Matthieß
abstract: Social constructions of groups shape policy and representation, but measuring them at scale remains difficult. Existing approaches typically face a trade-off between the number of social categories and the number of time points they cover. This article introduces SOCCAT, a multilingual pipeline for detecting and classifying social categorizations in large text corpora. SOCCAT first filters large corpora for sentences containing social category mentions and then uses Natural Language Inference to classify these mentions into 57 specific categories across eight broader classes related to socio-economic position, labor market position, age and family status, identities, professions, social roles, deviance, and real estate ownership. We train and validate the pipeline on manually annotated French and German newspaper data from 1995 to 2023. The pipeline performs strongly across countries, outlets, and time periods. Applying SOCCAT to more than 9 million newspaper articles, we show that it produces substantively plausible patterns of category salience and temporal variation around major focusing events, including migration debates and the Covid-19 pandemic. By releasing the manually annotated data and trained models, SOCCAT provides ready-to-use infrastructure for constructing comparable category-specific corpora and for downstream analyses of framing, deservingness, representation, and feedback effects between public discourse and policy.

title: From Free Access to Winner-Takes-All - A Reactive Sequence Perspective on Digital Platform Capitalism
abstract: This paper reinterprets the emergence of the digital winner-takes-all economy through Mahoney’s concept of the reactive sequence. Rather than treating digital concentration as the natural result of network effects, scale economies, or technological lock-in, it argues that these mechanisms became decisive only within a historically specific institutional trajectory. The early internet was constituted as a non-commercial sphere shaped by gift exchange, hacker ethics, open sharing, and community-oriented practices. As these norms diffused into mass usage, they were simplified into low willingness to pay and expectations of free access. Digital firms reacted by monetizing other market sides, especially advertisers, sellers, and platform-dependent businesses. This adaptation intensified network effects, weakened price differentiation, and privileged market leaders. Over time, observed concentration became generalized into a winner-takes-all expectation and institutionalized through venture-capital strategies, rapid scaling norms, and founder self-understandings. The essay extends this sequence to contemporary AI firms and their emerging wealth elites.

title: Does social media explain young people’s support for Palestine?
abstract: Young people in Britain are much more supportive of Palestine than older people. Here I examine whether this difference can be explained by young people’s greater use of social media. Analysing data from the British Election Study collected in the summer of 2024, I find that the direct effect of social media use explains less than 30% of the gap between people aged 18–25 and those aged 66+. So while social media does seem to play a role, other factors or mechanisms are needed to explain most of the gap.

title: The Hiring Value of Digital Micro-Credentials. Evidence from a Discrete Choice Experiment in Germany
abstract: Micro-credentials such as online certificates or digital badges have been promoted as flexible tools to enhance employability, especially given the growing demand for continuing education and training. Yet, little is known about how employers value such credentials in hiring decisions and whether they function as complements or substitutes for other qualification signals. Drawing on signaling and human capital theory as well as the digital divide literature, this study examines the hiring value of digital and in-person micro-credentials relative to formal qualifications and work experience. The empirical analysis is based on a pre-registered discrete choice experiment conducted with 1,380 human resource professionals in Germany, who evaluated 12,048 applicant profiles across 24 occupations requiring higher education. Results based on conditional logit models show that digital micro-credentials do not increase hiring probabilities and are valued significantly less than equivalent certificates obtained through in-person courses. This difference is independent of recruiters’ prior experience with digital micro-credentials and largely driven by trust in the signal quality of the two credential formats. We also find that in-person micro-credentials issued by universities improve hiring chances compared to other providers. Furthermore, among applicants with weaker field-of-study matches, in-person micro-credentials can enhance employability, indicating a partially substitutional signaling function, while this is not the case for digital micro-credentials. These findings suggest that in strongly institutionalized labor markets such as Germany, employers continue to prioritize established (micro-)qualifications over emerging digital forms. At the same time, this is evidence against a digital divide in terms of outcomes.

title: IranWar.ai: An Open-Source Event-Level Dataset of the 2026 US–Iran Conflict
authors: Jacob E. Thomas, Aigerim Alpysbekova, Eugene Osei Mensah, Nigel Masara, Prakhar Sharma
abstract: We present the IranWar.ai Event-Level Research Dataset, a structured, openly available record of the first 30 days of the 2026 US–Iran conflict (Operation Epic Fury, February 28–March 29, 2026). The dataset comprises 1,653 event-level observations across 48 variables, derived from 16 curated JSON data files that power the IranWar.ai public intelligence dashboard. Each observation represents a discrete, verifiable event: an airstrike on a named target, a retaliatory attack, a financial market data point, a daily casualty estimate by faction, a naval deployment, or a diplomatic development. Nine analytical domains are covered (military strikes, retaliations, financial markets, humanitarian impacts, diplomatic events, naval operations, cyber activity, general military, and contextual data) integrating open-source intelligence (OSINT) from more than 60 specific source streams, including CENTCOM, the IDF, ACLED, the Iranian Red Crescent, the IAEA, Bloomberg, and the IMO. Beyond its function as a research resource, this paper examines the dataset as an epistemic object. Assembled in part by AI research agents and carrying known imperfections, the dataset is released with full transparency about its own limitations, including interpolated values, unverified records, and probabilistic confidence ratings. We argue that this transparency constitutes a deliberate counter-technology: a structural intervention against the conditions of the information flood, which we frame as a dual-layer epistemic crisis comprising both a structural component (the volume, velocity, and synthetic origin of available data overwhelming the infrastructure available to evaluate it) and a strategic component (the deliberate production of disinformation by state and non-state actors). Radical transparency is necessary for both layers but, by itself, is sufficient only against the structural layer; the strategic layer additionally requires interpretive expertise that situates discrepancies as political facts rather than data-quality defects. We further examine the dataset's relationship to AI training pipelines, model collapse dynamics, and the degradation of provenance metadata across summarization and citation chains. To address the limits of any single research team's expertise, we set out a tentative, non-exhaustive research agenda and invite collaborators across political science, public health, economics, military studies, media studies, and information science. The live dashboard, codebook, source data, prompts, manifests, reproducible extraction script, and versioned research releases are publicly available at github.com/jethomasphd/WarTheater; scheduled research releases are monthly, with interim correction releases when warranted.

title: A real-effort task with endogenous work environment
authors: Pablo Branas-Garza, Antonio M. Durán-Rosal, Filippos Exadaktylos, ADRIANA GAVIRIA DUGAND, Cesar Mantilla, Carlos Ramos
abstract: Experimental economics studies using real-effort offer a better match with the field environment than stated-effort studies, incorporating the psychological aspects affecting effort provision. However, this literature does not analyze how subjects choose the environment in which they work but rather studies effort provision within an exogenously given setting. This paper introduces a new task in which the experimenter offers a menu of costly inputs levels that increase the chance of success, and the subject chooses the levels that define their working environment subject to a budget constraint. We present an application of this task when there is a shock to input prices. We further release an open-access platform, AREPA (Allocation-based Real Effort Production App), which allows researchers to access and set up the task for teaching and research purposes.

title: Criminal Order and Gendered Violence: Gang Control, State Repression, and Violence Against Women in Chicago
abstract: Women are increasingly the targets of gunfire in Chicago. Between 2010 and 2016, women made up roughly one in ten shooting victims. By 2022, the share had reached nearly one in six. Chicago's gang order fragmented after the late-1990s federal prosecutions, as super-gangs gave way to block-level cliques and the arrangements that had structured local violence eroded with them. That much is well established. I argue that this fragmentation reshaped who absorbs the resulting violence, and that women bear a rising share of it. Drawing on a panel of 77 community areas (2008–2024), Chicago Police Department crime and shooting records, and gang-territory maps, I show that what predicts violence against women in Chicago is not which gang controls a neighborhood. Folk Nation and People Nation territories record nearly identical female victimization rates. What predicts it is whether any stable territorial order exists at all. Where governance consolidates, reported domestic violence falls. Where multiple gangs compete, it rises. A difference-in-differences design covering the years after November 2015, bracketed by the Laquan McDonald dashcam release and the collapse of proactive policing, shows female shooting victims rising by about half and fatal shootings of women nearly doubling in high-gang community areas relative to low-gang ones. The rise cannot be pinned on the police pullback alone. The paper documents the pattern itself, a sustained and geographically concentrated rise in lethal violence against women that a literature trained on aggregate homicide has missed.

title: These Four First: A Robust Endogenous Baseline for Relational Event Sequences
abstract: Which endogenous interaction mechanisms belong in a baseline relational event model? We test for recurring endogenous patterns in 217 empirical interaction sequences spanning ten social domains, evaluating a twelve-term candidate set by forward AIC reduction. Four terms appear in the top-four selection path in over 60% of domains — interaction-order constraints (IOviolation, 94%), prior sender initiation (SndInitiation, 88%), rank-recency of the most recent reverse-dyad event (RecRecencyRank, 65%), and dyadic repetition (Repetition, 61%) — with substantially diminishing returns at step 4. Together they re- cover approximately 97% of the maximum AIC improvement achievable within this candi- date set and represent three distinct behavioral mechanisms: sequential ordering, dyadic persistence, and reverse-dyad recency. Notably absent from this baseline are imitation (preferential attachment driven by others’ targeting behavior) and triadic closure terms, despite their prominent roles in the network literature; both are absorbed by forward-dyad history once SndInitiation and Repetition enter the model. IOviolation’s dominance as the most consistently selected term reflects the near-universal importance of participation- shift norms across interaction settings, even those that are not conversational. The step-2 composition diverges ecologically: in high-visibility settings RecRecencyRank competes closely with IOviolation at step 2 (38% vs. 60%), while in low-visibility settings IOviola- tion dominates step 2 in 79% of domains. These four terms are not a sufficient model — institutional context, status, demographic attributes, and additional endogenous mech- anisms all shape interaction — but they are a consistent baseline that researchers can include with confidence before introducing substantive covariates. The cross-domain sta- bility of this four-term core — spanning private messaging, classroom interaction, and gang violence — hints at a compact set of dyadic mechanisms that may constitute near- universal building blocks of directed social interaction.

title: Neurodivergent Intelligence Framework: A Dimensional Framework for Understanding Cognition in Neurodivergent Profiles and Psychiatric Risk
abstract: Emerging evidence in neurodevelopmental research increasingly indicates that individuals diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) may demonstrate amplified pattern recognition capabilities as a core cognitive feature. Historically, these capacities have been framed as deficits within prevailing diagnostic and clinical traditions rather than as expressions of differentiated cognitive architecture. However, the field has yet to develop a framework that accounts for the distinct types of capabilities across these profiles or the developmental conditions that shape their expression. The present paper introduces the Neurodivergent Intelligence Framework (NIF), proposing that individuals within these diagnostic categories exhibit two broad pattern recognition orientations: one externally oriented, functioning primarily through real-time environmental input, and the other internally oriented, operating through subconscious and reflective processing. Within each orientation, the domain toward which pattern recognition is directed produces distinct processing combinations, yielding four total combinations with different functional strengths, neurochemical dependencies, and vulnerability profiles. These differences are proposed to produce differential responses to early developmental environments, with long-term risk emerging when those environments fail to meet the cognitive demands of the individual’s specific architecture. Beginning in early developmental windows, exposure to social dynamics, parenting styles, and institutional contexts may shape neuroplasticity in ways that either support or suppress these cognitive profiles, with suppression potentially producing neurochemical consequences across dopamine, serotonin, oxytocin, and cortisol systems. When this suppression becomes chronic across critical developmental periods, the framework proposes that resulting cognitive and neurochemical strain may increase risk for impulsivity-driven coping responses and psychiatric comorbidity. In cases where these suppressive patterns coincide with trauma exposure and prolonged environmental misalignment, the framework further proposes elevated risk for conditions including schizophrenia, OCD, and bipolar disorder. The NIF also identifies elevated substance use disorder risk as a downstream consequence of chronic architectural misidentification and unmet neurochemical demands.

title: Regeneration, Renewal and Digital Consumption in a Low-Income Community
authors: Billy Southern, Belinda Tyrell, Carl Hughes, Alan Southern
abstract: This article is based on research undertaken in a community in Huyton, Liverpool, where the authors explored how spatial inequality influences the way households use the internet. The work seeks to challenge current ideas about digital exclusion, particularly where access to technology is suggested to be a barrier, or that skills and motivation needed to be digitally connected are low. As we seek to understand digital connectivity in a low-income community, we aim to support more effective regeneration policy making, looking at how the market shapes consumption and what this can mean for public services. In our study neighbourhood, we record low-income levels and high levels of digital engagement. Around eight from ten households are fully connected, although they face disproportionately high internet costs, a poverty premium that is driven by commercial consumption shaped around retail and entertainment services. There is scant evidence to suggest that digital illiteracy and incompetence are barriers to use, although connection and consumption rarely translate into tangible benefits or formal qualifications. There is evidence of an imbalance between commercial digital consumption and low use of digital public services, meaning the main beneficiaries from digital consumption are private sector providers, and that specific groups such as the elderly are increasingly isolated from both digital and physical services. If regeneration policy in low-income communities remains committed to supply-side interventions, then disproportionate costs of digital connection need to be considered. Policies should not only seek to support the human capital of individuals but seek digital engagement in more calculated ways, building social capital and moving towards intuitive digital public services, providing a shift in perspective on digital skills, and the promotion of a more productive and equitable digital consumption. This work highlights the need for broader regeneration interventions that can see such digital engagement as strategic assets for productive use.

title: The Road Violence Toll of Urban Sprawl: A Registered Report of Collisions with Cyclists and Pedestrians in England and Wales
authors: Jose Pina-Sánchez, Caroline Tait, Roger Beecham, Juan Fonseca Zamora
abstract: The risk of road collision for cyclists and pedestrians is higher in sprawls than in city centres. As a result of commuting, we also suspect that the higher risk of collision caused by residents from car-dependent sprawls is not confined to their own areas but disproportionately imposed on city dwellers. However, due to the absence of direct comparisons we do not know the specific road violence imposed by urban sprawls. This registered report will combine collision data from the Department for Transport and the Urban Grammar Spatial Signatures dataset that characterises geography by form and function to estimate the share of road violence induced by sprawls’ residents within and outside their localities, and relative to levels of road violence generated by city dwellers in England and Wales. Furthermore, using the subset of collisions data including contributory factors, we will provide a lower bound estimate regarding the share of collisions that could be classified as motoring offences. Drawing on these findings we will reassess the widely held beliefs of: i) sprawls as safer than city centres; ii) residents from urban sprawls are more law-abiding than city dwellers; and iii) crime prevention strategies that promote car-dependent developments as effective.
